trajectory_summary: The call opens with a curious, warm customer and ends with a tentative in-person appointment — on the surface, a win. But the appointment is structurally fragile: the bill was never collected, a callback was promised for the next morning, Mildred's Saturday schedule is crowded (haircut, granddaughter visit, stairlift installation), and three vulnerability disclosures went unacknowledged. Parker moved the call forward by following Mildred's lead rather than steering it — the booking exists because Mildred was cooperative, not because Parker built it.
outcome_dispute: The appointment should be tagged 'conditional/fragile' rather than a clean book. The bill was never collected — a callback was scheduled for Friday morning to get it, with Mildred's granddaughter as the intermediary. Mildred's Saturday is already occupied with a haircut, a granddaughter visit, and a stairlift installation (05:23: 'The guy's coming tomorrow to remap the set'). The 5PM slot was chosen by Mildred as a workaround, not a committed time. No button-up elements were completed: no one-hour block stated, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promised. The appointment exists but has multiple failure vectors.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'm so tired, and I think I'm gonna fall down. I was getting ready to lay down when you called.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Mildred disclosed health and mobility vulnerability three times across this call (04:01, 05:45, 09:09) and received no acknowledgment on any of them. The closer will walk into an appointment with a customer who has extended trust three times without receiving it back — meaning the closer must rebuild that trust layer from scratch before the proposal can land. Parker's pattern of bypassing vulnerability disclosures is not a one-call issue; it is a structural gap that will repeat on every call with an elderly or health-compromised customer. The manager should use this call to introduce coaching_philosophy.empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure and establish a floor standard: every vulnerability disclosure gets a named acknowledgment before the rep moves to the next beat.

key_moments.quote: I assume you do you have AEP or AES?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker skips the interest_in_solar verify item entirely — the schema's highest-weighted emotional anchor — and jumps directly to utility company. The verify section's coaching note is explicit: 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR. Depth matters more than the answer. Top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Mildred had already signaled bill pain ('I thought that was probably a joke' about the no-cost claim) and would have been receptive to this question. The skip is reactive — Parker is moving through logistics rather than building emotional alignment.

key_moments.quote: it's a townhouse, and it's in a condominium association.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker never asks who else is on the title. Mildred is 84, on a walker, has a helper who comes one day a week, and her granddaughter is visiting from Florida. The adult_child_decision_maker protocol is potentially applicable — if a family member is on the title or functionally co-decides, the appointment is structurally vulnerable without them present. Parker's HOA team mention (02:00) addresses the association question but the title/co-owners item is never touched.

key_moments.quote: They have a what now? I can't hear good, honey. And I've got my hearing aids in, but I think the batteries are dead.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mildred discloses a hearing impairment mid-call — batteries dead in her hearing aids. Parker's response is 'Oh, no worries' and then repeats the credit question more slowly. The communication accommodation is adequate, but the disclosure itself — an elderly woman alone, hearing aids failing, about to lie down — is a trust gift that receives no acknowledgment. This is the first of three vulnerability disclosures that go unacknowledged across the call.

key_moments.quote: I'm so tired, and I think I'm gonna fall down. I was getting ready to lay down when you called.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the second vulnerability disclosure — physical exhaustion, near-collapse, interrupted rest. Parker's response is to pivot immediately to scheduling availability: 'I know we have some availability as early as, like, tomorrow or Saturday.' The disclosure is treated as a scheduling constraint rather than a human moment. Per the schema, the correct move is acknowledge specifically, then bridge to value — not skip to logistics.
